name: Three-Dimensional Geometry — Points in Space
whatIsIt: A flat page uses two numbers (x, y). Real space needs THREE: (x, y, z) — left/right, forward/back, and up/down. Three axes meet at the ORIGIN and split space into 8 regions called OCTANTS. The distance of a point (x, y, z) from the origin is √(x² + y² + z²) — Pythagoras in 3D. The distance between two points adds the squared differences of all three coordinates.
whyNeeded: The world is 3D: GPS gives latitude, longitude AND altitude; games, robotics, architecture and physics all locate objects with three numbers. 3D coordinates are the foundation for vectors and 3D graphics.
realLifeUses: GPS position: latitude, longitude and altitude
A drone's position in the air (x, y, height)

vocabulary.meaning: Three numbers that fix a point in space.
One of the 8 regions the three axes split space into (like quadrants, but in 3D).
√(x² + y² + z²) — Pythagoras extended to three dimensions.
The three number lines (x, y, z) that meet at right angles at the origin.

story.title: The Drone's Position
story.text: Rohan's drone hovers 2 metres east, 3 metres north and 6 metres up from the launch pad. 'How far is it in a straight line?' asks Coach. Rohan uses 3D Pythagoras: distance = √(2² + 3² + 6²) = √(4 + 9 + 36) = √49 = 7 metres. 'Three numbers to place it, and one square root for the distance!' Coach nods.
story.extractedProblem: A drone is at (2, 3, 6). How far is it from the origin?

visual.caption: A point in space needs three numbers (x, y, z). The dashed line to the origin has length √(2²+3²+6²) = √49 = 7.

teachingGallery.title: Three numbers, one distance
teachingGallery.note: Add the squares of all three coordinates, then square-root.

examples.caption: 3D distance is Pythagoras with three squares under the root.
The three axes cut space into 8 octants (2D had 4 quadrants).

standardMethod.summary: Locate a point with (x, y, z). Distance from the origin = √(x² + y² + z²). Between two points, take the difference in each coordinate, square, add, and square-root.
steps: Write the point as three coordinates (x, y, z).
Distance from the origin: square each coordinate, add, take the square root.
Between two points: subtract matching coordinates, square each difference, add, square-root.
Remember there are 3 axes and 8 octants.
Check the answer is positive (a distance is never negative).

workedExamples.problem: Find the distance of (2, 3, 6) from the origin.
How many octants does 3D space have?
steps: Three axes each split space in two.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using only two coordinates (x, y) to locate a point in space.
commonMistakes.fix: Space needs THREE coordinates (x, y, z). Two numbers only fix a point on a flat plane.

commonMistakes.mistake: Reporting x² + y² + z² as the distance without the square root.
commonMistakes.fix: Distance = √(x² + y² + z²). For (2,3,6): the sum is 49, but the distance is √49 = 7.

easy.q: How many numbers (coordinates) are needed to fix a point in space?
How many octants does 3D space have?
hintLadder: Three axes, each splitting in two.

challenge.q: To locate a drone in the air, Ravi uses only (x, y). What's the fix?

options.label: Add height z → use (x, y, z)

challenge.answer: Add height z → use (x, y, z)
hintLadder: The drone is up in the air.

questions.q: Coordinates needed for a point in space?
The 3D distance from the origin is…
hintLadder: Root of the sum of squares.

teachBackPrompt: Use a corner of a room as the origin for a grown-up and give a point three numbers (along two walls and up). Show the straight-line distance is √(x²+y²+z²), e.g. (2,3,6) → √49 = 7.

revisionCard.summary: Space needs 3 coordinates (x, y, z). Three axes meet at the origin and make 8 octants. Distance from origin = √(x²+y²+z²) (Pythagoras in 3D). Between two points: square the differences of all three coordinates, add, square-root. Always take the root; a distance is positive.
